localBiteReport: Inshore action on Choctawhatchee Bay has been solid this week, with speckled trout leading the way — anglers are reporting good numbers and a few quality fish mixed in. Bull redfish are also showing up in a big way; one angler out of Santa Rosa Beach reported a 40¼-inch, 26-lb bull this week. Flounder are being found alongside reds and trout on bay trips, and redfish have also been spotted cruising the beaches. With water temps at 84°F, some wind has pushed anglers to be selective about where they fish, but trout are scattered around the bay and still feeding well on soft plastics and live shrimp.

aiSummary: Choctawhatchee Bay is fishing well this week with speckled trout leading a strong inshore bite, bull redfish making a notable showing, and flounder rounding out the mix on bay trips. Today brings sunny skies, a manageable south wind of 5 to 10 mph, and a negligible 3% storm chance before 1 PM, so conditions are largely favorable — though the 90°F high and heat index up to 102°F mean anglers should plan to fish early and stay hydrated. Water temps are sitting at 84°F with slightly stained clarity, so lean toward live shrimp and natural-colored soft plastics to help fish locate the bait.

bridge.aiNote: The 331 Bridge spans a narrow neck of Choctawhatchee Bay, creating a current funnel that pushes bait through on both tides and concentrates speckled trout, redfish, sheepshead, and black drum around the pilings year-round. The lee-side pilings are the prime ambush zone on incoming tides, where fish stack up to intercept passing bait. Being open water, this is the first spot on the bay to become unfishable when wind and chop build.
bridge.todaysCall: The south wind at 5 to 10 mph will generate some chop on this exposed stretch, but it should remain manageable for most boats — position on the lee side of the bridge to stay out of the roughest water. Low tide falls around 12:52 PM, so the morning incoming tide window before that is your best setup, with current funneling bait through the pilings right as the sun climbs. With slightly stained water, go with a scented soft plastic or live shrimp tight to the pilings to help trout and reds zero in. Plan to be off the water or tucked in before the heat index peaks in the early afternoon.

alaqua.aiNote: Alaqua Bayou offers protected water with a strong year-round redfish population, plus trout and flounder near the mouth, where oyster bars serve as the primary ambush zone. Tree cover along the upper creek keeps water temperatures a few degrees cooler than the open bay and provides reliable shelter from wind and weather. The upper creek transitions to brackish water and can hold bass alongside smaller redfish.
alaqua.todaysCall: The tree-lined banks make Alaqua a smart pick on a hot, sunny day like today — the shade keeps water temps slightly cooler and keeps fish from going lockjaw in the midday heat. Fish the oyster bars at the mouth on the morning incoming tide before low water hits at 12:52 PM, then work back into the creek as the tide drops and concentrates bait in the bends. Slightly stained water calls for a chartreuse or gold soft plastic on a light jig head, or free-lined live shrimp along the oyster edges. Redfish are showing well bay-wide this week, and this bayou's protected population should be no different.

basin.aiNote: Basin Bayou sees less fishing pressure than nearby Alaqua and LaGrange, which generally means less-educated fish and a more willing bite. Interior grass flats are classic sight-fishing territory for redfish in under two feet of water, and pompano occasionally show up at the mouth on moving tides in late spring and early summer. It is somewhat less protected than LaGrange when weather builds across the bay.
basin.todaysCall: Lower pressure here is a real advantage on a clear, sunny day when fish in busier bayous may get lockjaw from boat traffic — the sight-fishing on the interior grass flats can be exceptional when the sun is high and conditions let you spot tails and wakes. Hit the mouth on the incoming tide this morning while current is moving, then shift to the shallow flats as the tide drops toward the 12:52 PM low and pushes reds onto the grass edges. Slightly stained water means sight-fishing will require good polarized optics and patience, but it's very doable. Soft plastics in natural shrimp colors or a weedless gold spoon are good choices for shallow-flat reds today.

lagrange.aiNote: LaGrange Bayou is considered the top trout producer among the bayous on this side of the bay, with legendary oyster bars at the mouth and interior grass flats that hold redfish year-round. Deep holes inside the bayou shelter black drum and sheepshead, adding diversity to the catch. It is the most protected water on this side of Choctawhatchee Bay, making it the reliable go-to when weather builds.
lagrange.todaysCall: With the waxing gibbous moon providing a moderate tidal push, the oyster bars at the LaGrange mouth should see solid current on the morning incoming tide — get there early and work those bars hard with live shrimp or a paddle-tail soft plastic before the low tide at 12:52 PM slows things down. Trout have been scattered and feeding well across the bay this week, and LaGrange's flats are historically one of the most consistent producers, so expect good numbers if you're there at first light. The slight water stain actually works in your favor at this spot, keeping fish less spooky on the shallow flats. Even if the wind picks up to the higher end of the 5–10 mph south range, this bayou's protected layout keeps conditions comfortable.

fourmile.aiNote: Four Mile Creek is a tidal creek running between Shipyard Marina and the bay, where incoming tides push baitfish up the creek and concentrate redfish and trout in the bends and along grass edges. The creek mouth is a prime flounder zone, and the upper section transitions to brackish water that can hold bass alongside smaller reds. Short and well-sheltered, it is the safest fishery on the dashboard when weather threatens.
fourmile.todaysCall: Starting right from Shipyard Marina, Four Mile Creek is the most convenient option for an early start — get in on the incoming tide this morning and work soft plastics or live shrimp through the bends and grass edges while bait is being pushed up the creek. Flounder have been reported as part of the bay-wide mix this week alongside reds and trout, so don't overlook the creek mouth with a slow-dragged gulp or live finger mullet. The south wind at 5 to 10 mph barely matters inside the creek's protected corridor, making this a comfortable choice even if conditions feel rough out on the open bay. With the low tide arriving at 12:52 PM, plan to shift toward the mouth before the water drops completely and the upper creek shallows out.

hogtown.aiNote: Hogtown Bayou is a signature Santa Rosa Beach guide spot and one of the most productive trout flats on the bay, with summer mornings being peak time as trout stack on grass in two to four feet of water. Spanish mackerel appear at the mouth on moving tides, adding a bonus target for anglers who work the outer area. It sees regular fishing pressure but produces consistently, and its distance from the 331 Bridge means factoring in extra run time.
hogtown.todaysCall: Summer mornings on Hogtown's grass flats are about as good as it gets for speckled trout on Choctawhatchee Bay — be on the water at or just after the 5:56 AM sunrise to capitalize on the low-light window before the sun gets high and bright overhead. The waxing gibbous moon is providing a moderate tidal push, so current will be moving on the flats ahead of the 12:52 PM low tide, which is exactly when trout feed aggressively on soft plastics and live shrimp. Bay-wide trout reports have been excellent this week with good numbers and quality fish, and Hogtown's reputation as a top producer suggests it should be firing this morning. Keep a faster presentation ready at the mouth — Spanish mackerel can show on moving tides, and a small spoon or D.O.A. Baitbuster worked quickly through the current can get bit.

rocky.aiNote: Rocky Bayou sits within Fred Gannon Rocky Bayou State Park near Niceville, offering a protected, scenic, and significantly less pressured fishery than the bayous closer to the 331 Bridge. Freshwater influence from Rocky Creek enhances flounder fishing, and the glass-calm state park coves are well-suited for sight-fishing redfish. Mangrove snapper hold near dock pilings, making this one of the more species-diverse spots on the dashboard.
rocky.todaysCall: The run to Rocky Bayou from Shipyard Marina is the longest of any spot on the dashboard, so plan your departure to arrive at or near sunrise and take full advantage of the early morning low-light bite before the heat index climbs toward 102°F. The protected state park coves will be glass-calm despite the south wind, making sight-fishing conditions for redfish excellent — polarized glasses and a stealthy approach on the shallow flats are your best tools. Flounder fishing should be productive given the freshwater influence from Rocky Creek; work a slow-dragged soft plastic or live finger mullet along the transition zones where fresh and salt water meet. Mangrove snapper near the dock pilings are a reliable mid-morning option once the sun gets up and the sight-fishing window on the flats narrows.
